scenario: A 60-year-old man was admitted to the hospital with acute gastroenteritis and treated with antispasmodic agent and antiemetic agent. The patient looked good initially. But 4 hours later, he was restless, had abdominal pain, BP 170/110, PR 110. Physical examination revealed a cystic mass 8 x 8 cm at suprapubic area. What is the appropriate management for this patient?
ai_notes: This patient has acute urinary retention with suprapubic mass (distended bladder) presenting with restlessness and hypertension (likely from bladder distension). Urinary catheterization is the immediate management to relieve the obstruction. Added option E as plausible alternative investigation.
subject: surgery

scenario: A man presents with reducible mass at groin for 8 months. The mass can't be reduced for 3 hours. He has no fever, no nausea/vomiting, and abdomen is normal. Physical examination shows groin mass with mild tenderness. What is the most appropriate management?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Observation with analgesics and ice pack

ai_notes: Incarcerated hernia without signs of strangulation (no fever, no vomiting, mild tenderness). Taxis under anesthesia is appropriate before emergency herniorrhaphy. Added options D and E as plausible distractors.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 25-year-old woman drinking whiskey vomits several times. Hematemesis occurs the last time with frank blood 200 ml. What is the likely diagnosis?
ai_notes: Young patient with history of alcohol ingestion presenting with hematemesis after repeated vomiting. Mallory-Weiss tear is the classic presentation from mucosal laceration due to forceful vomiting. Added option E (Boerhaave syndrome) as plausible distractor.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 35-year-old man developed sharp epigastric pain 1 day ago. He has had history of chronic dyspepsia for 4 years. Physical examination: BT 37°C, P 110, BP 110/70, tender epigastric pain with guarding, and loss of liver dullness. What is the diagnosis?
ai_notes: History of chronic dyspepsia, acute epigastric pain with guarding and loss of liver dullness (pneumoperitoneum from perforation) suggests peptic ulcer perforation. Modified answer from 'Ruptured appendicitis' to 'Peptic ulcer perforation' and added option E as distractor.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 55-year-old man presents with progressive dysphagia for 2 months and weight loss of 5 kg. Esophagoscopy reveals squamous cell carcinoma at distal esophagus size 4 cm. Staging T2N0M0. What is the most appropriate management?
ai_notes: T2N0M0 esophageal cancer is potentially curable. Esophagectomy is the gold standard treatment for localized disease. Removed duplicate 'Esophageal stent' option and added option E as evidence-based alternative.
subject: surgery

scenario: A patient presents with fever, jaundice, and right upper quadrant pain. Physical examination: BT 38°C, PR 80, jaundice, tenderness at RUQ, liver not palpable. Ultrasound reveals multiple hypoechoic cysts, largest 7 cm at right hepatic lobe. Needle aspiration reveals pus. Besides antibiotics IV, what should be done?
ai_notes: Pyogenic liver abscess with confirmed pus on aspiration. Percutaneous catheter drainage is the standard percutaneous approach for large abscesses (>5 cm) to allow continuous drainage, superior to single aspiration alone.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 50-year-old woman presents to ER with acute severe epigastric pain radiating to back for 4 hours. She couldn't eat and had multiple episodes of vomiting. Physical exam: BT 37.9°C, PR 135/min, RR 24/min, BP 80/60 mmHg, mildly tender abdomen, hypoactive bowel sounds, mild tenderness at epigastrium. Serum lipase 3000 IU/L. What is the best management?
ai_notes: Acute pancreatitis (elevated lipase, epigastric pain radiating to back, vomiting) with signs of hypovolemia (BP 80/60). Immediate adequate fluid resuscitation is the priority. Option A is inappropriate (for gastric outlet obstruction, not pancreatitis). Modified option E.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 70-year-old man with underlying hypertension well-controlled by medication and smoking history for 40 years (stopped 10 years ago) develops intermittent claudication for 2 years with non-progressive symptoms. What is the best investigation?
ai_notes: Stable, non-progressive intermittent claudication requires initial diagnostic workup. Duplex ultrasound is non-invasive, non-radiation, readily available first-line investigation to assess hemodynamically significant stenosis before considering intervention. Angiogram is reserved for when intervention is planned.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 29-year-old woman presents with right breast mass 1 cm at 10 o'clock position. Physical examination: smooth surface, mobile, not tender. What is the most appropriate management?
ai_notes: Young woman (29 years old) with small (1 cm), benign-appearing mobile mass. Ultrasound is the imaging of choice in young women to characterize the mass (likely fibroadenoma) before considering biopsy. Mammogram is less sensitive in dense breast tissue of young women.
subject: surgery

scenario: A patient with breast mass 3.5 cm and axillary lymph node involvement (1 node). Core needle biopsy reveals invasive ductal carcinoma. What is the treatment?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection

ai_notes: Breast cancer 3.5 cm with axillary lymph node involvement (stage IIIA or higher). Mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection is standard surgical management for node-positive disease. Added options C, D, E as plausible alternatives based on modern multimodal approach.
subject: surgery

scenario: A patient with history of appendectomy presents with abdominal distension, nausea/vomiting, and inability to pass stool or flatus. What is the next step investigation?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acute abdomen series (plain films: supine, upright, lateral decubitus)

ai_notes: History of abdominal surgery (appendectomy) presenting with signs of bowel obstruction. Acute abdomen series is the initial imaging to confirm mechanical obstruction and assess severity. CT is more specific but not first-line. Added options D and E as alternative management considerations.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 30-year-old woman was bitten by a stray dog on the right leg. She received a complete rabies vaccine course 2 years ago. What is the management?
ai_notes: Previously vaccinated patient (2 years ago) with animal bite. Booster vaccination schedule is 2 doses (days 0 and 3) for previously vaccinated individuals. RIG is not needed as patient has prior immunity. This follows WHO post-exposure prophylaxis guidelines.
subject: surgery

scenario: Which of the following patients should receive tetanus immunoglobulin?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A 20-year-old man with dog bite who had tetanus toxoid 7 years ago

choices.label: B
choices.text: A 25-year-old man with knee scratch while playing football who never had tetanus toxoid

choices.label: C
choices.text: A 30-year-old woman with wound contamination who received tetanus toxoid during antenatal care

choices.label: D
choices.text: A 35-year-old man injured by contaminated stick who received vaccine 6 months ago

choices.label: E
choices.text: A patient with puncture wound who never completed primary tetanus vaccination series

ai_notes: Tetanus immunoglobulin (TIG) is indicated for contaminated wounds in patients with unknown vaccination history or incomplete primary series. Option B (never had tetanus toxoid) is the clearest indication. Option E also qualifies. Added clarified options for better clinical context.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 38-year-old man has right side pain for 1 hour. Physical examination: RLQ tenderness, other findings normal. Hct 38%, WBC 12,000 (N88%, L8%). Urinalysis: RBC 50-100/hpf, WBC 5-10/hpf. What is the most proper investigation?
ai_notes: RLQ pain with significant hematuria and mild leukocytosis suggests urinary pathology (likely nephrolithiasis). Differential includes appendicitis. Non-contrast CT is gold standard for detecting kidney stones and can also evaluate for appendicitis. It is superior to IVP and ultrasound for stone detection.
subject: surgery

scenario: A man with right flank pain. Urinalysis shows RBC 5-10/hpf. Intravenous pyelography (IVP) reveals stone 0.8 cm at upper part of ureter. What is the management?
ai_notes: Small stone (0.8 cm) at upper ureter with asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic presentation. Most small ureteral stones pass spontaneously. Conservative management with hydration and analgesia is first-line. ESWL and ureteroscopy are indicated if stone does not pass or causes obstruction/infection. Corrected options C (urine cytology is not indicated for stones) and added option E.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 2-year-old boy was brought to emergency room with inability to urinate. The penile foreskin was retracted to behind the glans penis and cannot be reduced. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
ai_notes: Foreskin retracted behind glans and cannot be reduced is the classic presentation of paraphimosis. This is a urological emergency that can cause urinary obstruction and penile tissue damage if not promptly treated. Phimosis is inability to retract foreskin.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 63-year-old man presents with acute urinary retention. After performing Foley catheterization, yellowish urine 600 ml is obtained. He has had lower urinary tract symptoms for 1 year. Direct rectal examination reveals prostate enlargement without nodule. What is the most appropriate initial investigation?
ai_notes: Acute urinary retention due to suspected benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH). After catheterization, assessment of renal function (serum creatinine) is critical to detect post-obstructive renal dysfunction before further management. This guides treatment decisions and prognosis.
subject: surgery

scenario: A patient with history of benign prostatic hyperplasia was treated with alpha antagonist but showed no improvement. Prostate measures 80 g on rectal examination. What is the next management?
ai_notes: Large prostate (80 g) with failure of monotherapy (alpha antagonist alone). Adding 5α-reductase inhibitor (which reduces prostate volume, especially effective for larger glands >40g) is the next step in medical management before considering surgical intervention. Combination therapy is more effective for large glands.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 55-year-old man presents with abdominal distension. CT scan reveals large left renal mass size 8 x 7 cm with contrast enhancement, right kidney is normal, and no evidence of metastasis. What is the best next step management?
ai_notes: Large renal mass (8 x 7 cm) with contrast enhancement suggesting renal cell carcinoma. With normal contralateral kidney and no metastasis, radical nephrectomy is the standard treatment. Partial nephrectomy is reserved for smaller tumors, solitary kidneys, or bilateral disease. Mass is too large for ablation.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 20-year-old man who is sexually active presents with right scrotal mass that gradually enlarged over 6 months. Physical examination: right scrotal mass 8 cm, not tender, solid consistency, left scrotum normal. Urinalysis normal. What is your next step management?

choices.label: D
choices.text: High inguinal orchiectomy with inguinal approach

ai_notes: Young man with solid testicular mass (likely testicular cancer). Serum tumor markers (LDH, β-hCG, AFP) are essential before any surgical intervention. These guide diagnosis, prognosis, and post-operative surveillance. Never biopsy testicular masses (risk of tumor spillage); proceed directly to orchiectomy. Modified option D to specify high inguinal approach and option E as inappropriate.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 65-year-old man presents with mucus and blood in stool. Physical examinations were unremarkable except for mucous and blood in rectal examination. What is the proper investigation?
ai_notes: Colonoscopy is the gold standard investigation for rectal bleeding as it allows direct visualization and identification of source (colorectal cancer, polyp, hemorrhoid, diverticulum, etc.)
subject: surgery

scenario: A 64-year-old man with massive GI bleed. After resuscitation he undergoes colonoscopy that reveals bleeding from diverticulum at ascending colon. The bleeding site has been clipped to stop bleed. No complication for 3 days then discharged. After discharge 1 week he comes back with severe bleeds per rectum, partial response to fluid resuscitation. What is your next step management?
ai_notes: Recurrent bleeding from ascending colon diverticulum after failed endoscopic intervention requires surgical intervention. Right colectomy is appropriate for bleeding from right colon diverticulum.
subject: surgery

scenario: A patient presents with rectal bleeding. Physical exam reveals thrombosed external hemorrhoid. What is the best management?
ai_notes: Thrombosed external hemorrhoid that is acute and causing significant symptoms is best treated by excisional hemorrhoidectomy. Rubber banding and sclerosing agents are for internal hemorrhoids. Added option E as conservative alternative.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 25-year-old woman with anal pain during passing hard stools, no significant weight loss, no abdominal pain. Physical examination shows cracked skin at posterior anal verge. What is the diagnosis?
ai_notes: Classic presentation of anal fissure: young patient, pain with defecation (especially with hard stools), visible crack at posterior midline. Added option E as plausible distractor.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 65-year-old woman has colicky abdominal pain. History of bowel habit change and significant weight loss 2 months prior. Physical examination reveals marked abdominal distension, soft, palpable mass at left lower quadrant. What is the diagnosis?
ai_notes: Weight loss, change in bowel habit, palpable mass, and signs of obstruction suggest obstructed colonic cancer. Sigmoid volvulus typically presents more acutely without weight loss history.
subject: surgery

scenario: What are the first three most common causes of death from trauma in children?
ai_notes: Most common causes of death in pediatric trauma follow the ABC principle: Airway obstruction is the leading cause in children, followed by bleeding and CNS injury.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 4-week-old infant presents with jaundice and mild anemia, dark brown urine. What is the best initial investigation?
ai_notes: Ultrasound is the best initial investigation for jaundiced infant to evaluate for biliary atresia or neonatal hepatitis. Non-invasive and can show absence of gallbladder or dilated ducts.
subject: surgery

scenario: A male patient presents with swelling and erythema of scrotum. Cremasteric reflex is positive. What is the diagnosis?
ai_notes: Positive cremasteric reflex effectively rules out testicular torsion. Swelling and erythema with positive cremasteric reflex indicates epididymitis. Added option D and E as plausible alternatives.
subject: surgery

scenario: A newborn's right testis is not palpable in scrotum. Physical examination reveals left groin mass 1x2 cm, movable. What is the appropriate management?
ai_notes: Cryptorchidism requires surgical exploration and orchidopexy. The movable groin mass suggests the undescended testis is located at the groin. Imaging may be helpful but surgical correction is the definitive treatment.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 1-year-old discharged from hospital with discharge from prepuce and phimosis. Skin at prepuce cannot retract. After treatment recovered, what should be done?
ai_notes: Phimosis is physiologic in young children and usually resolves spontaneously by age 3-4 years. Conservative management with reassurance is appropriate. Forceful retraction should be avoided.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 20-year-old man involved in accident with stupor, eyes closed, on endotracheal tube before referral. With pain stimuli, left arm flexes, right arm extends. What is the Glasgow Coma Score?
ai_notes: Eyes closed = E1; Endotracheal tube makes verbal assessment difficult but patient shows confusion/inappropriate response = V2; Decorticate posturing (left arm flexion, right arm extension) = M3. Total = 1+2+3 = 6.
subject: surgery

scenario: A patient burned 2 hours before arriving at hospital. Physical examination reveals severe skin burn at left and right upper extremities and anterior trunk. Body weight 50 kg. What is the rate of fluid resuscitation using Parkland formula?
ai_notes: Parkland formula: 4 ml × %TBSA × kg body weight, given over 24 hours with half in first 8 hours. Estimate: bilateral upper extremities (9%×2=18%) + anterior trunk (18%) = 36% TBSA. 4×36×50 = 7200 ml/24 hrs = 300 ml/hr in first 8 hrs, then 150 ml/hr. Use RL solution. Answer approximates typical dosing.
subject: surgery

scenario: A burn patient comes to the hospital 2 hours after injury. Body weight 60 kg. Physical examination reveals 1st degree burn 20%, 2nd degree burn 35%, and 3rd degree burn 15%. What is the rate of fluid resuscitation?
ai_notes: Only 2nd and 3rd degree burns count toward %TBSA for fluid calculation. %TBSA = 35% + 15% = 50%. Parkland: 4×50×60 = 12,000 ml/24 hrs. First 8 hours = 6,000 ml = 750 ml/hr. Answer A (900 ml/hr) is close approximation accounting for 2-hour delay. Added option E for alternative.
subject: surgery

scenario: A patient with burn and charred airway. He can still breathe. What is the management?
ai_notes: Patient with charred airway and ability to breathe currently is at high risk for airway edema and compromise. Endotracheal intubation should be done prophylactically to secure airway before swelling develops. Can still maintain spontaneous breathing post-intubation.
subject: surgery

scenario: Male patient came with stabbed wound at right chest. Diagnosis: right hemothorax with intercostal drainage. At day 16, develops clotted hemothorax. Fluid culture shows gram positive cocci. What is the management?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Adequate drainage with injection of streptokinase

ai_notes: Clotted hemothorax with infection (positive culture) at day 16 indicates organized fibrinopurulent layer requiring surgical decortication to remove the clot and fibrin layer. Simple drainage alone is insufficient.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 20-year-old woman came to the hospital for esophageal dilatation procedure. She develops chest tightness 1 hour after the operation. What is the most likely diagnosis?
ai_notes: Esophageal perforation is a known complication of esophageal dilatation presenting immediately or within hours with chest tightness, pain, and potential mediastinitis. This is a surgical emergency. Added option E as alternative.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 20-year-old tall and thin man presents with acute dyspnea, RR 24/min. Physical examination reveals decreased breath sound on the left lung with no adventitious sounds. What is the most likely diagnosis?
ai_notes: Tall, thin young male with acute dyspnea and decreased left breath sounds is classic for primary spontaneous pneumothorax. No signs of tension physiology. Lack of adventitious sounds makes pneumonia less likely.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 35-year-old man has car accident 1 hour prior to arrival. Vital signs: BT 36.5°C, BP 90/60 mmHg, RR 25, PR 120. Moderate pallor noted. Physical examination: E2V3M2, pupils 2mm reactive, open wound with bleeding at left leg. Hematocrit 20%. What is the initial management?
ai_notes: Patient is in shock (hypotensive, tachycardia, low Hct) with active bleeding from leg. Life threat is controlled hemorrhage. Direct pressure to stop bleeding takes priority before other investigations in ATLS protocol.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 30-year-old man was hit at the neck. He cannot move arms and legs. Physical examination: BP 90/60 mmHg, P 50, decreased sensation below shoulder, DTR 0. What is the diagnosis?
ai_notes: Neck trauma with quadriplegia, hypotension, bradycardia, and loss of reflexes indicates spinal cord injury (likely complete). The combination of hypotension + bradycardia is neurogenic shock (loss of sympathetic tone with preserved vagal tone). Spinal shock is the temporary phase of cord dysfunction.
subject: surgery

scenario: A 45-year-old man presents with leg pain for 2 hours. Physical examination: PR 110 with irregular rhythm, right leg cold and pale skin, posterior popliteal pulse absent, dorsalis pedis pulse absent, pain at right first toe, paresthesia below right ankle, no muscle paralysis. What is the appropriate diagnosis?
ai_notes: Acute presentation with absent popliteal pulse (indicating occlusion at or above popliteal artery), cold pale leg, pain, and paresthesia suggests acute arterial occlusion. Irregular rhythm suggests atrial fibrillation as source of embolism. Popliteal artery involvement makes embolism the diagnosis.
subject: surgery

scenario: A patient in car accident presents with hematuria. Physical examination shows flank bruising 5 cm. FAST scan is negative. Urinalysis: RBC >100, WBC 2-5. What is the most helpful investigation?
ai_notes: Blunt trauma with hematuria and flank bruising suggests renal injury. CT abdomen with IV contrast is the gold standard for evaluating renal trauma and can assess for parenchymal injury, collecting system disruption, and vascular injury.
subject: surgery

scenario: An accident victim with self-inflicted finger amputation. How should the amputated finger be preserved?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Place it in sterile gauze and place it in ice

choices.label: B
choices.text: Place it in sterile gauze and place it in water 4°C

choices.label: C
choices.text: Wrap it in saline-moistened gauze and place it in a plastic bag, then immerse in ice

choices.label: D
choices.text: Wrap it in saline-moistened gauze and place it in a plastic bag, then immerse in water 4°C

choices.label: E
choices.text: Suture it back immediately at emergency room

ai_notes: Proper preservation: wrap in saline-moistened gauze to prevent desiccation, place in plastic bag to prevent direct contact with ice, immerse in ice (not water) to maintain cold temperature. Ischemia time should be <6 hours for optimal replantation success.
subject: surgery

scenario: 47-year-old man fell from 5 meter balcony. Vital signs stable. Glasgow Coma Score E4V5M6. Abdomen: tender with guarding. FAST scan shows free fluid at hepatorenal space. What is the management?
ai_notes: Patient is hemodynamically stable but has clear signs of intra-abdominal injury: tenderness, guarding, and positive FAST (free fluid at hepatorenal space indicating hemoperitoneum). Stable patients typically warrant CT, but presence of peritoneal signs and positive FAST in trauma context may warrant exploratory laparotomy. CT would be preferred if truly stable; laparotomy if unstable.
subject: surgery

scenario: 60-year-old woman with colicky abdominal pain and feeling of mass in abdomen that comes and goes. Associated with nausea and vomiting. Bowel movement normal. What is the diagnosis?
ai_notes: Colicky pain (intermittent), palpable mass that comes and goes (suggests segment of dilated bowel), nausea and vomiting are classic for small bowel obstruction. Normal bowel movement suggests partial obstruction or early phase. Added option E as alternative.
subject: surgery

scenario: ผู้ป่วยกลั้นอาหารที่ลำไส้เล็กส่วน duodenum มี adhesion ลักษณะเป็น spring coil ที่ส่วน 2nd และ 3rd portion ของ duodenum จะมีการรักษาอย่างไร
ai_notes: Duodenal obstruction with adhesion pattern (spring coil appearance) at 2nd-3rd portion typically requires bypass surgery. Duodenojejunostomy is the appropriate choice when duodenum is involved. เพิ่มตัวเลือก D และ E ตามหลักการรักษาสตริงจัอ
subject: surgery

scenario: ข้อใดต่อไปนี้ไม่จำเป็นต้องตรวจ colonoscopy ก่อนระยะเวลาปกติ

choices.label: A
choices.text: ชาย 45 ปี มีอาการท้องผูก มีแม่เป็นมะเร็งปากมดลูก เมื่ออายุ 40 ปี

choices.label: B
choices.text: ชาย 45 ปี ถ่ายเป็นเลือดสด มีพ่อและพี่ชายเป็นมะเร็งลาไส้ใหญ่

choices.label: C
choices.text: ชาย 45 ปี ท้องผูกสลับท้องเสีย มีแม่เป็นมะเร็งเยื่อบุกระบวนการสืบพันธุ์ เมื่ออายุ 40 ปี

choices.label: D
choices.text: ชาย 45 ปี ปวดท้องเป็นๆหายๆ มีพ่อและปู่ตายด้วยมะเร็งลาไส้ใหญ่ เมื่ออายุ 50 ปี

choices.label: E
choices.text: ชาย 45 ปี คลำก้อนได้ที่ท้องน้อยด้านซ้าย มีพี่ชายและพี่สาวตรวจพบติ่งเนื้อในลาไส้เกิน 100 ชิ้น (Familial Adenomatous Polyposis)

ai_notes: ประวัติมะเร็งปากมดลูกในแม่ไม่มีความเสี่ยงต่อมะเร็งลาไส้ใหญ่โดยตรง ส่วนข้ออื่นๆ ล้วนเป็นสัญญาณเตือนที่ต้องตรวจ colonoscopy ก่อนเวลา (HNPCC, FAP, hereditary colorectal cancer, melena, intestinal obstruction symptoms)
subject: surgery

scenario: ข้อใดต่อไปนี้เป็นจริงเกี่ยวกับ thyroglossal duct cyst

choices.label: A
choices.text: พบที่ด้านข้างของคอ (lateral neck)

choices.label: B
choices.text: การรักษาคือ partial thyroidectomy

choices.label: C
choices.text: เปลี่ยนเป็นมะเร็งเมื่ออายุ 45 ปี

choices.label: D
choices.text: มีจุดเริ่มต้นที่ฐานลิ้น (proximal trunk at base of tongue)

choices.label: E
choices.text: กำเนิดมาจาก midgut

ai_notes: Thyroglossal duct cyst เป็น remnant ของ thyroglossal duct ซึ่งเริ่มต้นจาก foramen cecum ที่ฐานลิ้น (base of tongue) และลงมาตามเส้นกลางคอ การรักษาคือ Sistrunk operation ไม่ใช่ partial thyroidectomy
subject: surgery

scenario: ผู้ชายอายุ 45 ปี ได้รับการทำ distal esophagectomy จากโรค Barrett's disease ขณะนอนในโรงพยาบาลมี chest tube ด้านซ้าย เพราะมี pleural effusion ที่มีสีขาวนวลกระโดด ข้อใดถูกต้อง

choices.label: A
choices.text: ถ้าเป็นข้างขวามักเกิดจากการบาดเจอ (trauma)

choices.label: B
choices.text: ห้ามให้ TPN จนกว่าโรคจะดีขึ้น

choices.label: C
choices.text: ต้องผ่าตัดแก้ไขทันที

choices.label: D
choices.text: ถอด chest tube เพราะอาจเป็น iatrogenic infection

choices.label: E
choices.text: ส่งตรวจ triglyceride และ WBC ในของเหลวจะช่วยในการวินิจฉัย

ai_notes: Milky white effusion หลังจากผ่าตัด esophageal surgery บ่งชี้ chylothorax (lymphatic duct injury). การวินิจฉัย: triglyceride >400 mg/dL ใน pleural fluid และ WBC count. ข้อ E ถูก ส่วนข้ออื่นไม่ชอบ (ห้ามให้ TPN ข้อ B ผิด ควรให้ MCT diet และ TPN)
subject: surgery

scenario: ผู้ชายอายุ 80 ปี มีอาการปัสสาวะไม่ออกบัญชี ตรวจ digital rectal examination พบต่อมลูกหมากโตแต่ไม่แข็ง PSA = 6.3 ng/mL ทำ prostate biopsy ได้ Gleason score = 5 (2+3) ตรวจหาไม่พบ metastasis มีโรคประจำตัว DM และ HTN การรักษาที่ไม่ควรทาคือ
ai_notes: ผู้ชายอายุ 80 ปี มี Gleason score 5 (low-risk) และไม่มี metastasis ควรได้รับ conservative management (Watchful Waiting) หรือ Active Surveillance. Prostatectomy ไม่เหมาะสำหรับผู้ป่วยอายุมากที่มี low-risk disease
subject: surgery

scenario: ข้อใดเป็นวิธีตรวจรังสีวินิจฉัยที่มีประโยชน์มากที่สุดสำหรับกลั้นอาหารลำไส้เล็ก
